registry.notes: Added 2026-03-17: identified via STEP Bible testing during Col 2:8 exegetical review. 'Energy' as a translation gloss for energeia/energēs covers the active-working dimension of power — used in the NT for both divine working within the inner person AND Satanic active working. This dual usage makes it particularly significant for understanding what operates on the inner being. Primary Greek terms expected: G1753 energeia (active energy/working — Phil 3:21 divine working; 2 Thess 2:9,11 Satanic working; Eph 1:19; Col 1:29), G1754 energeō (to work/be active — Gal 2:8; 5:6), G1756 energēs (effective/active — 1 Cor 16:9; Heb 4:12 — the word of God as energēs). Medium confidence: 'energy' may not be a common translation gloss in all versions — Session A should verify which English translations use this term and identify the primary gloss. If 'energy' proves too narrow as an entry point, may need to fold into #196 power with a sub-note. Cross-links: power (#196), transformation (#161), spiritual powers (#195).
registry.description: Energy is the capacity for active work and effort — the inner drive and outer force that enables doing. The Greek vocabulary here (energeia) covers the working activity that produces results. In the New Testament it appears primarily in theological contexts: the energy at work in the believer is God's energy working in and through them (Colossians 1:29). Human energy is real but is sustained and directed by divine energy: it is the working out of what God works in. [XREF: covered by power (#196).]

terms.medium_def:
working, power, energy 
energy, efficacy, power, Phil. 3:21; Col. 2:12; active energy, operation, Eph. 1:19; 3:7; 4:16; Col. 1:29; 2Thess. 2:9, 11*
terms.lsj_entry:
ἐνέργ-εια, ἡ, 
activity, operation, opposed to ἕξις (disposition), [Refs 4th c.BC+]; opposed to ἀογία, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]; physiological function, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]; performance, τῶν καθηκόντων [Refs 1st c.AD+]; activity, of drugs, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]; force, of an engine, [Refs 1st c.BC+] (but, mechanism, 'action', [Refs 2nd c.BC+]
__b workmanship, Aristeas [Refs]
__2 especially of divine or supernatural action, [NT+3rd c.AD+]; magical operation, ἱερὰ ἐ. [Refs]
__3 plural, ἐνέργειαι cosmic forces, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]
__4 Grammars, active force, opposed to πάθος, [Refs 2nd c.BC+]; ἐνέργειαι καὶ πάθη active and passive forms, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]
__5 Rhet., vigour of style, [Refs 4th c.BC+]
__II in the philosophical of [Refs 4th c.BC+] actuality, Metaphorical[Refs]; opposed to ὕλη, [Refs]; ἡ ὡς ἐ. οὐσία, substance in the sense of actuality, [Refs]; opposed to ἐντελέχεια, as actuality to full reality, [Refs]; ἐνεργείᾳ actually, opposed to δυνάμει, [Refs]

terms.medium_def:
to be at work in; to produce active 
to effect, 1Cor. 12:6, 11; Gal. 3:5; Eph. 1:11; Phil. 2:13; to put into operation, Eph. 1:20; absolute to be active, Mt. 14:2; Mk. 6:14; Eph. 2:2; in NT to communicate energy and efficiency, Gal. 2:8; passive or middle to come into activity, be actively developed; to be active, be in operation, towards a result, 2Cor. 4:12; 2Thess. 2:7; to be an active power or principle, Rom. 7:5; 1Thess. 2:12; instinct with activity; in action, operative, 2Cor. 1:6; Gal. 5:6; Eph. 3:20; Col. 1:29; earnest, Jas. 5:16
terms.lsj_entry:
ἐνεργ-έω, 
to be in action or activity, operate, [Refs 4th c.BC+]; especially of divine or supernatural action, frequently in NT., τινί, ἔν τινι, [NT]:—middle, [NT]
__2 to be efficacious, of drugs, [Refs 1st c.AD+]; to be effective, of troops, ταῖς σαρίσαις [Refs 2nd c.AD+]:—passive, to be the object of action, [Refs 4th c.BC+]; also, to be actualized, [Refs 3rd c.AD+]
__II transitive, effect, execute, πάντα κατὰ δύναμιν [Refs 2nd c.BC+]:—passive, to be actively carried on, ὁ πόλεμος ἐνηργεῖτο [Refs 2nd c.BC+]things executed, [Refs 2nd c.BC+]
__III in Medicine texts, of sexual intercourse, τὴν τοιαύτην πρᾶξιν -οῦσι μετρίως [Refs] in opere esse, [Refs 3rd c.BC+]
__IV operate, in surgery, [Refs 4th c.AD+]; ὁ ἐνεργῶν, ὁ ἐνεργούμενος, the surgeon, the patient, [Refs 2nd c.AD+]

ἐνεργ-ής, ές, 
later form of ἐνεργός, active, effective, μηχανὰς ἐνεργεῖς ποιοῦντες [Refs 1st c.BC+]; of medicines, strong, [Refs 1st c.AD+] more effective, πρός τινα [Refs 4th c.BC+]: superlative -έστατος, πρός τι [Refs 1st c.BC+]
